title: Sangamo exits stage - Nature Biotechnology
gist: Genome-editing pioneer Sangamo Therapeutics filed for bankruptcy in June 2026, selling its AAV delivery, zinc finger and integrase technology plus prion program to Eli Lilly for $50 million and its lead Fabry disease gene therapy isaralgagene civaparvovec to Astellas for $25 million, after three decades and multiple clinical-stage programs without a single approval.

insights.claim: Sangamo Therapeutics filed for bankruptcy in June 2026 while simultaneously securing asset-purchase agreements with two pharmaceutical companies to preserve value in some of its technology and pipeline.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A structured bankruptcy paired with pre-arranged asset sales is a template other cash-strapped clinical-stage genome-editing companies may follow rather than liquidating outright.

insights.claim: Eli Lilly bid $50 million for Sangamo's AAV delivery system, its zinc finger and integrase technologies, and its prion disease program.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A $50 million price for an entire platform (delivery tech, editing IP, and a disease program) from a distressed seller shows large pharma can now acquire genome-editing platform capabilities cheaply out of bankruptcy rather than paying premium valuations for going concerns.
insights.quote: Eli Lilly bid $50 million for the company’s AAV delivery system, its zinc finger and integrase technologies, and its prion program

insights.claim: Astellas bid $25 million for Sangamo's most advanced clinical program, the Fabry disease gene replacement therapy isaralgagene civaparvovec.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Even Sangamo's most clinically advanced asset fetched only $25 million, underscoring how depressed valuations have become for late-preclinical/clinical gene therapy assets absent a partner or pivotal-trial readout.
insights.quote: Astellas bid $25 million for the Fabry disease gene replacement therapy isaralgagene civaparvovec, the company’s most advanced clinical program

insights.claim: Despite launching in 1995 around a foundational zinc finger nuclease patent thicket and advancing multiple assets into clinical trials, no Sangamo program ever reached a pivotal trial or won regulatory approval.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A cautionary case study for investors in platform-technology-first biotechs: owning foundational IP and clinical-stage optionality is not sufficient without a program that reaches pivotal data and approval.
insights.quote: Sangamo launched in 1995 with what was then a groundbreaking technology and took multiple assets into clinical trials, but none advanced into pivotal trials, let alone approval.

insights.claim: Sangamo can be credited with moving gene therapy away from replacing entire genes toward making precise, targeted changes within the genome, and in 2017 it became the first company to perform gene editing directly inside a living human, using its SB-913 therapy for mucopolysaccharidosis type 2 (Hunter syndrome).
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Sangamo's scientific firsts (precision editing concept, first in vivo human gene editing) retain historical and IP value even as the company itself failed commercially — explaining why Lilly paid specifically for the platform IP rather than the whole company.
insights.locator: Sangamo was formed around a patent thicket
